original_abstract: Text classification serves as a cornerstone for numerous natural language processing applications. While traditional classifiers heavily depend on manual feature engineering—such as human-defined dictionaries, knowledge bases, and complex tree kernels—these methods often struggle to capture deep semantic nuances and suffer from data sparsity. In this paper, we propose a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) designed to learn text representations without the need for handcrafted features. Our architecture utilizes a bi-directional recurrent structure to capture broad contextual information, which introduces significantly less noise than traditional window-based neural networks. This is followed by a max-pooling layer that automatically identifies and extracts the most influential words for classification. We evaluated our model across four diverse datasets, covering topic, sentiment, and writing style classification in both English and Chinese. The experimental results demonstrate that the RCNN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, showing particular strength in handling document-level text.
humanized_abstract: Text classification is a fundamental problem in natural language processing (NLP). Most text classification algorithms rely on human engineered features, such as dictionaries, knowledge bases or tree kernels, which cannot effectively explore the underlying semantic relationships between words and also suffer from the data sparsity issue. In this paper, we propose a novel recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) model, which learns high quality features automatically without requiring any manual engineering. With the bi-directional recurrent neural network (RNN) to learn the global context at the word-level with less noise, the max-pooling is employed to automatically select the most relevant words for text classification. Experimental results are conducted on four benchmark text classification datasets for different tasks at different levels, such as topic classification, sentiment classification, and style classification in both English and Chinese. The results show that our proposed RCNN consistently achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art text classification algorithms, especially for document-level text classification.

Experimental results are conducted on four benchmark text classification datasets for different tasks at different levels, such as topic classification, sentiment classification, and style classification in both English and Chinese. The results show that our proposed RCNN consistently achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art text classification algorithms, especially for document-level text classification.
